other_observers: Riley Morris, Heather Morris, Jim Beatty, Rich Donley, Amy Dobbins, Jan Rees, John Rees; Yes, I was the original finder and identifier of the Northern Parula.
specific_location: The bird was seen along the Animas River Trail in Durango at 17 acre park site. The location is along the stretch of the river that is east of the intersection of CR 211 and US 160/US 550. The bird was seen from the parking lot behind Four Corners River Sports and upstream as much as 100 meters from there.
description: I was searching for a Northern Parula because I was hearing him sing. I located the very small warbler by pinpointing the direction of the song. The bird was a dark, bluish-gray color on the head, upperparts and upperside of tail. The chin, throat, and chest were yellow. The eye was surrounded by an upper and lower, white eye arc. The lores were black. The yellow chest was marked with a broad chest band which was deep red in color. The red band was bordered by a narrower black band above. The belly and undertail coverts were white. I noticed the green patch on the back several times. The bird had two white wing bars; the upper wing bar was more prominent than the lower one.Behaviors: This warbler was actively feeding in the foliage of cottonwood trees. The activity of the warbler suggested that he was gleaning insects from the leaves.Call: He sang a buzzy song that was somewhat similar to a Black-throated Gray Warbler, except it was faster and ascending. In addition he sang an ascending trill, followed by a note or two.Plumage: adult
